data.content: Now let me look at the core module and also the env files for security configuration clues:

content.text:
| MCP:I7emSV | # Spring Boot Template
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | A production-ready template for Spring Boot projects with modern best practices and built-in infrastructure.
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ![Docker Compose](https://imgur.com/qWLYDrg.png)
| MCP:I7emSV | ![Swagger UI](https://imgur.com/5MixfZe.png)
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Features
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Spring Boot 4.0.0** (Java 25)
| MCP:I7emSV | - **UUID Primary Keys** via Hibernate `@UuidGenerator`
| MCP:I7emSV | - **JWT Authentication & Authorization** with refresh tokens
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Role-based Security** with Spring Security
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Spring Boot Actuator** for health, info, and metrics endpoints
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Request Correlation ID** — automatic `X-Correlation-ID` header propagation and MDC logging
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Structured JSON Logging** — human-readable console + JSON file output (Logstash format)
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Pagination** — built into `BaseService` and controllers via Spring Data `Pageable`
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Rate Limiting** — annotation-based (`@RateLimit`, `@RateLimitCategory`) with configurable categories
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Idempotency** — `@Idempotent` annotation with Redis-backed request deduplication
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Swagger UI** for API documentation ([http://localhost:8080/api/v1/swagger-ui/index.html](http://localhost:8080/api/v1/swagger-ui/index.html))
| MCP:I7emSV | - **PostgreSQL**, **Redis**, **Kafka & Zookeeper** — all containerized via Docker Compose
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Multi-stage Dockerfile** with dependency caching and JRE runtime
| MCP:I7emSV | - **JaCoCo Code Coverage** reports
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Maven Enforcer** — ensures Java 25+ and Maven 3.8.7+
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Lombok** and **MapStruct** for reduced boilerplate
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Environment Isolation** — run dev, test, and prod in parallel via Docker Compose
| MCP:I7emSV | - **Unit & Integration Tests** for services, controllers, and infrastructure
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | > **Note:** Java 25 and Maven 3.8.7+ are required.
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Quick Start
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ### 1. Use This Template
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | Click **"Use this template"** on GitHub, then clone your new repository:
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```sh
| MCP:I7emSV | git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/<your-repo>.git
| MCP:I7emSV | cd <your-repo>
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ### 2. Initialize Your Project
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | Run the initialization script to rename packages, modules, and containers to match your project:
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```sh
| MCP:I7emSV | ./init-project.sh --group-id com.acme --artifact my-app --name "My App" --author "Your Name"
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | | Option | Description | Required |
| MCP:I7emSV | |--------|-------------|----------|
| MCP:I7emSV | | `--group-id` | Maven groupId / Java package root (e.g. `com.acme`) | Yes |
| MCP:I7emSV | | `--artifact` | Artifact name in kebab-case (e.g. `my-app`) | Yes |
| MCP:I7emSV | | `--name` | Human-readable project name (default: derived from artifact) | No |
| MCP:I7emSV | | `--author` | Author name for `@author` tags (default: `tzesh`) | No |
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | You can also run it interactively — omit the flags and you'll be prompted:
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```sh
| MCP:I7emSV | ./init-project.sh
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | The script will:
| MCP:I7emSV | - Rename Java packages (`com.tzesh.springtemplate` -> your package)
| MCP:I7emSV | - Rename module directories (`spring-boot-template-api` -> `<artifact>-api`, etc.)
| MCP:I7emSV | - Update Docker container names, Kafka group IDs, and all references
| MCP:I7emSV | - Replace `@author` tags if `--author` is provided
| MCP:I7emSV | - Self-delete after completion
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ### 3. Verify the setup
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```sh
| MCP:I7emSV | ./mvnw clean install
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ### 4. Start infrastructure and run
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```sh
| MCP:I7emSV | # Start PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka
| MCP:I7emSV | docker compose up -d db redis zookeeper kafka
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Run the application
| MCP:I7emSV | ./mvnw -pl spring-boot-template-api spring-boot:run
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | Or run everything with Docker:
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```sh
| MCP:I7emSV | docker compose up -d
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | The application will be available at [http://localhost:8080/api/v1/swagger-ui/index.html](http://localhost:8080/api/v1/swagger-ui/index.html).
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Project Structure
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | SpringBootTemplate/
| MCP:I7emSV | ├── pom.xml                          # Parent POM (enforcer, JaCoCo, shared deps)
| MCP:I7emSV | ├── spring-boot-template-core/       # Core module (base classes, annotations, exceptions)
| MCP:I7emSV | │   └── src/main/java/.../base/
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── annotation/              # @RateLimit, @Idempotent, etc.
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── dto/                     # BaseDTO
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── entity/                  # BaseEntity
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── exception/               # NotFoundException, RateLimitExceededException, etc.
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── handler/                 # @ControllerAdvice exception handlers
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── mapper/                  # BaseMapper (MapStruct)
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── response/                # BaseResponse wrapper
| MCP:I7emSV | │       └── service/                 # BaseService (CRUD + pagination)
| MCP:I7emSV | ├── spring-boot-template-api/        # API module (controllers, services, config)
| MCP:I7emSV | │   ├── Dockerfile                   # Multi-stage build
| MCP:I7emSV | │   └── src/main/java/.../
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── config/
| MCP:I7emSV | │       │   ├── correlation/         # CorrelationIdFilter
| MCP:I7emSV | │       │   ├── idempotency/         # IdempotencyAspect
| MCP:I7emSV | │       │   ├── ratelimit/           # RateLimitAspect, RateLimitingFilter
| MCP:I7emSV | │       │   └── security/            # SecurityChain, JWT filters
| MCP:I7emSV | │       ├── controller/              # REST controllers
| MCP:I7emSV | │       └── service/                 # Business services
| MCP:I7emSV | ├── docker-compose.yml               # PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, app
| MCP:I7emSV | ├── init-project.sh                  # Project initialization script
| MCP:I7emSV | ├── .editorconfig                    # Editor formatting rules
| MCP:I7emSV | ├── .gitattributes                   # Git line ending config
| MCP:I7emSV | └── .dockerignore                    # Docker build exclusions
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## API Usage
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | 1. **Register** a new user via `POST /auth/register`
| MCP:I7emSV | 2. **Login** via `POST /auth/login` to obtain a JWT token
| MCP:I7emSV | 3. **Authorize** in Swagger UI using the `Authorize` button
| MCP:I7emSV | 4. **Access** secured endpoints (e.g. user management requires `ADMIN` role)
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Actuator Endpoints
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | Health, info, and metrics are exposed at:
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | | Endpoint | Description |
| MCP:I7emSV | |----------|-------------|
| MCP:I7emSV | | `GET /api/v1/actuator/health` | Application health status |
| MCP:I7emSV | | `GET /api/v1/actuator/info` | Application info |
| MCP:I7emSV | | `GET /api/v1/actuator/metrics` | Application metrics |
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | Health details are shown when authenticated with an authorized role. Kubernetes liveness and readiness probes are enabled.
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Correlation ID
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | Every request is assigned a correlation ID for distributed tracing:
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | - If the client sends an `X-Correlation-ID` header, it is reused
| MCP:I7emSV | - Otherwise, a UUID is generated automatically
| MCP:I7emSV | - The correlation ID is added to the MDC for all log output and returned in the response header
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Rate Limiting
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ### Annotation-based (per-endpoint)
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```java
| MCP:I7emSV | @RateLimit(limit = 20, duration = 1, key = RateLimitKeyStrategy.USER)
| MCP:I7emSV | @RateLimitCategory(RateLimitCategoryType.STANDARD)
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ### Configurable categories in `application.properties`
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | | Category | Default Limit | Duration |
| MCP:I7emSV | |----------|--------------|----------|
| MCP:I7emSV | | `strict` | 10 req | 1 min |
| MCP:I7emSV | | `standard` | 60 req | 1 min |
| MCP:I7emSV | | `relaxed` | 200 req | 1 min |
| MCP:I7emSV | | `authentication` | 5 req | 1 min |
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Idempotency
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | Annotate mutating endpoints with `@Idempotent` to prevent duplicate processing. Clients must send an `Idempotency-Key` header. Backed by Redis.
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```java
| MCP:I7emSV | @PostMapping("/")
| MCP:I7emSV | @Idempotent
| MCP:I7emSV | public ResponseEntity<BaseResponse<UserDTO>> createUser(...) { ... }
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Kafka Messaging
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | - Kafka and Zookeeper are included in Docker Compose
| MCP:I7emSV | - `POST /kafka/send` sends messages to Kafka (supports optional scheduled delivery)
| MCP:I7emSV | - Consumer logs received messages
| MCP:I7emSV | - Logging level configurable in `application.properties`
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Environment Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | Use `.env` files for environment-specific variables and Docker Compose override files for port/resource separation:
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```sh
| MCP:I7emSV | # Run test environment
| MCP:I7emSV | docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.test.override.yml --env-file .env.test -p myapp_test up -d
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Run prod environment
| MCP:I7emSV | docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.override.yml --env-file .env.prod -p myapp_prod up -d
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Code Coverage
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | JaCoCo reports are generated during `mvn test` and available at:
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | spring-boot-template-api/target/site/jacoco/index.html
| MCP:I7emSV | spring-boot-template-core/target/site/jacoco/index.html
| MCP:I7emSV | ```
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## Contributing
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for setup instructions, code style, and PR process.
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ---
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | ## License
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | See [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.

| MCP:I7emSV | ## Base Configuration File for Spring Boot Template API
| MCP:I7emSV | # ==========================
| MCP:I7emSV | # This file contains the base configuration for the Spring Boot Template API.
| MCP:I7emSV | # It includes settings for server, application, database, JPA, and JWT.
| MCP:I7emSV | # ==========================
| MCP:I7emSV | # Note: This file is intended to be used as a template. Modify the values as needed for your environment.
| MCP:I7emSV | # ==========================
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Spring Boot Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | server.port=8080
| MCP:I7emSV | server.servlet.context-path=/api/v1/
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Application Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | application.title=Spring Template
| MCP:I7emSV | application.version=2.0
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # JPA Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # JWT Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | security.jwt.secret-key=34743777217A25432A462D4A614E645267556B58703273357638782F413F4428472B4B6250655368566D597133743677397A244226452948404D635166546A57
| MCP:I7emSV | security.jwt.authorization-key=614E645267556B58703273357638792F423F4528482B4B6250655368566D597133743677397A24432646294A404E635166546A576E5A7234753778214125442A
| MCP:I7emSV | security.jwt.expiration=86400000
| MCP:I7emSV | security.jwt.refresh-expiration=604800000
| MCP:I7emSV | security.jwt.issuer=SpringTemplate
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Rate Limit Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.enabled=true
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.limit=100
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.duration-minutes=1
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Rate Limit Category Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.strict.limit=10
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.strict.duration-minutes=1
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.standard.limit=60
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.standard.duration-minutes=1
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.relaxed.limit=200
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.relaxed.duration-minutes=1
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.authentication.limit=5
| MCP:I7emSV | ratelimit.authentication.duration-minutes=1
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Redis Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.data.redis.host=redis
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.data.redis.port=6379
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Spring Session with Redis
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.session.store-type=redis
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.session.redis.namespace=spring:session
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.session.timeout=30m
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Kafka Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.kafka.bootstrap-servers=kafka:9092
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.kafka.consumer.group-id=spring-template-group
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.kafka.consumer.auto-offset-reset=earliest
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.kafka.consumer.key-deserializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.kafka.consumer.value-deserializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.kafka.producer.key-serializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer
| MCP:I7emSV | spring.kafka.producer.value-serializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Kafka Logging Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | logging.level.org.apache.kafka=INFO
| MCP:I7emSV | logging.level.org.springframework.kafka=INFO
| MCP:I7emSV | # To enable debug logging for troubleshooting, use DEBUG instead of INFO
| MCP:I7emSV | # logging.level.org.apache.kafka=DEBUG
| MCP:I7emSV | # logging.level.org.springframework.kafka=DEBUG
| MCP:I7emSV | 
| MCP:I7emSV | # Actuator Configuration
| MCP:I7emSV | management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics
| MCP:I7emSV | management.endpoint.health.show-details=when-authorized
| MCP:I7emSV | management.endpoint.health.probes.enabled=true

data.content: Let me also look at the user management controller:

Now I have a thorough understanding of the entire security architecture. Here's the comprehensive explanation:

---

## Security & Protected API Usage in This Template

This Spring Boot template implements a **multi-layered security architecture** built on Spring Security with JWT-based stateless authentication. Here's how it all fits together:

---

### 1. JWT Authentication

**Configuration & Properties** (`application.properties`):
```
security.jwt.secret-key=34743777217A25432A462D4A614E645267556B58703273357638782F413F4428472B4B6250655368566D597133743677397A244226452948404D635166546A57
security.jwt.authorization-key=614E645267556B58703273357638792F423F4528482B4B6250655368566D597133743677397A24432646294A404E635166546A576E5A7234753778214125442A
security.jwt.expiration=86400000        # 24 hours (access token)
security.jwt.refresh-expiration=604800000  # 7 days (refresh token)
security.jwt.issuer=SpringTemplate
```

**`JwtService`** handles all token operations:
- Uses **HMAC-SHA** signing (via `io.jsonwebtoken` / JJWT library) with a Base64-decoded secret key
- Generates two token types: **access tokens** (short-lived, 24h) and **refresh tokens** (long-lived, 7 days)
- Tokens carry the **username as the subject claim**, plus standard `issuedAt`, `expiration`, and `issuer` claims
- Validates tokens by extracting the username, matching it against `UserDetails`, and checking expiration

---

### 2. The Authentication Filter Pipeline

**`AuthenticationFilter`** (extends `OncePerRequestFilter`) runs before `UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter`:

1. **Skip auth endpoints** — Requests to `/api/v1/auth/**` bypass the filter entirely
2. **Extract Bearer token** — Looks for `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header
3. **Validate the token** — Extracts the username, loads `UserDetails` from the database via `UserDetailsService`, then checks:
   - JWT signature and expiration (via `JwtService.isTokenValid`)
   - Token is **not revoked or expired in the database** (via `TokenRepository.findByToken`)
4. **Set security context** — Creates a `UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken` with the user's authorities and stores it in `SecurityContextHolder`

This is a **stateless, token-revocation-aware** design: the JWT is cryptographically verified *and* checked against the database for revocation status.

---

### 3. Token Persistence & Revocation

**`TokenEntity`** stores every issued JWT in PostgreSQL:

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `token` | The raw JWT string (unique) |
| `type` | Always `BEARER` |
| `revoked` | Flag for manual revocation |
| `expired` | Flag for expiration tracking |
| `user` | FK to the owning `User` |

**Key behaviors:**
- On **login**, all previous valid tokens for that user are **revoked** (single-session enforcement)
- On **logout** (`LogoutService`), the current token is marked `revoked=true` and `expired=true`
- The `AuthenticationFilter` rejects any token that is revoked or expired in the database, even if the JWT itself hasn't cryptographically expired

---

### 4. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

**Three roles** defined in `Role.java`:

| Role | Permissions |
|------|-------------|
| `USER` | *(none — basic authenticated access)* |
| `MANAGER` | `management:read`, `management:create`, `management:update`, `management:delete` |
| `ADMIN` | All manager permissions + `admin:read`, `admin:create`, `admin:update`, `admin:delete` |

Each role also gets a `ROLE_<NAME>` authority (e.g., `ROLE_ADMIN`), which is what `hasRole('ADMIN')` checks.

**Authorization is enforced at two levels:**

#### a) URL-level (SecurityChain)
```java
// Permit all (no auth needed)
"/auth/**", "/swagger-ui/**", "/actuator/**"

// Role-based on /management/**
"/management/**" → hasAnyRole(ADMIN, MANAGER)
GET  "/management/**" → hasAnyAuthority(ADMIN_READ, MANAGER_READ)
POST "/management/**" → hasAnyAuthority(ADMIN_CREATE, MANAGER_CREATE)
// ... etc for PUT, PATCH, DELETE

// Everything else
.anyRequest().authenticated()
```

#### b) Method-level (`@PreAuthorize`)
Enabled via `@EnableMethodSecurity`. Used in controllers, e.g.:
```java
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")     // Only ADMIN can create/list/delete users
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') || hasRole('USER') || hasRole('MODERATOR')")  // Authorize endpoint
```

The **`UserController`** requires `ADMIN` role for all CRUD operations except:
- `GET /users/current` — any authenticated user can view their own profile
- `PATCH /users/current` — any authenticated user can update their own profile

---

### 5. User Interaction Flow

Here's how a user is expected to interact with secured endpoints:

#### Step 1: Register
```
POST /api/v1/auth/register
{
  "username": "alice",
  "email": "alice@example.com",
  "name": "Alice",
  "password": "securePassword123"
}
```
**Response:** Returns `accessToken` + `refreshToken`. New users are assigned the `USER` role by default.

#### Step 2: Login
```
POST /api/v1/auth/login
{
  "username": "alice",
  "password": "securePassword123"
}
```
**Response:** Returns fresh `accessToken` + `refreshToken`. All previous tokens for this user are revoked.

#### Step 3: Access Secured Endpoints
Include the JWT as a Bearer token:
```
GET /api/v1/users/current
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUz...
```

#### Step 4: Refresh Token
```
POST /api/v1/auth/refresh-token
Authorization: Bearer <refresh_token>
```
**Response:** A new access token (old one is revoked).

#### Step 5: Logout
```
POST /api/v1/auth/logout
Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
```
Revokes the token in the database and clears the security context.

#### Step 6: Role Elevation (Admin-only)
```
POST /api/v1/auth/authorize
{
  "username": "alice",
  "role": "ADMIN",
  "secret": "<authorization-key-from-config>"
}
```
Requires the `security.jwt.authorization-key` secret to prevent unauthorized role changes. This is a privileged operation protected by both a shared secret and `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') || hasRole('USER') || hasRole('MODERATOR')")`.

---

### 6. Additional Security Features

| Feature | Implementation |
|---------|---------------|
| **CSRF** | Disabled (stateless JWT, no cookies for auth) |
| **Session** | `STATELESS` — no server-side sessions |
| **Password hashing** | `BCryptPasswordEncoder` |
| **Rate limiting** | Auth endpoints: 5 req/min; Standard: 60/min; Strict: 10/min; Relaxed: 200/min |
| **Idempotency** | `@Idempotent` annotation on mutating endpoints (e.g., register, user creation) backed by Redis |
| **Unauthenticated errors** | Custom `AuthenticationEntryPoint` returns structured JSON `401` responses |
| **Swagger/Actuator** | Open endpoints (`permitAll`) but actuator health details only shown when authenticated with authorized role |

---

### Summary

The template provides a **production-grade JWT authentication and RBAC system** where:

1. **Users self-register** → receive JWT tokens
2. **All requests** carry `Bearer` tokens in the `Authorization` header
3. **Tokens are validated** both cryptographically and against the database (revocation check)
4. **Roles** (`USER`, `MANAGER`, `ADMIN`) control access via URL patterns and `@PreAuthorize` annotations
5. **Admins can elevate roles** using a protected `/auth/authorize` endpoint with a shared secret
6. **Token lifecycle** is fully managed: login issues tokens, refresh rotates them, logout revokes them
